name: Madhusudana Sarasvati
dates_notes: Late Mughal period; dated from manuscripts and contemporary references.

lineage_polemical.note: Madhusūdana's Advaita-Siddhi is the structural reply to Vyāsatīrtha's Nyāyāmṛta; the two together form the definitive Advaita–Dvaita dialectical exchange.
Madhusūdana stands within the Advaitin defense against the Madhva-school polemic, especially the Mithyātvānumāna-Khaṇḍana / Māyāvāda-Khaṇḍana attacks.

Madhusūdana's late Advaita has documented contact with the Trika tradition: the integration of bhakti-rasa, the developed treatment of pūrṇāhantā / sākṣi-ānanda, and the absorption of the rasa-camatkāra apparatus into the Bhakti-Rasāyana stand within reach of the Pratyabhijñā / Trika synthesis Abhinavagupta consummates.

core_thesis: Madhusūdana Sarasvatī brings the Vivaraṇa-Advaita line to its highest dialectical pitch under direct pressure from Vyāsatīrtha's *Nyāyāmṛta*. He inherits the standard architecture — Brahman as locus of *bhāva-rūpā-anirvacanīya-avidyā*, *jīva* as *cidābhāsa*, *anirvacanīya-mithyātva* of the world established by formal *anumāna* — and reconstructs it under hostile examination. The opening *paricheda* of the *Advaita-Siddhi* sets out and defends four successive definitions of *mithyātva*: (i) being the absolute non-existence of itself in its own substrate (*sva-āśrayatva-niṣṭha-atyantābhāva-pratiyogitva*); (ii) being the counter-positive of an absence locatable in the substrate of itself; (iii) being other than the real (*sad-vilakṣaṇatva*) without being the unreal; (iv) being the object of the cancelling cognition that arises with *brahma-jñāna*. Each definition is offered in reply to a distinct Vyāsatīrtha objection, and each is defended in turn; the cumulative point is that *mithyātva* is an *anirvacanīya* property reachable by multiple non-equivalent characterisations, none of which collapses *mithyā* into *asat*. The resulting three-generation exchange — Madhusūdana, Vyāsatīrtha, and Brahmānanda Sarasvatī's *Laghu-Candrikā* in Madhusūdana's defence — is one of the high-water marks of Indian philosophical debate, with both sides preserved and read together to this day. A second contribution stands beside the dialectical one. In the *Bhakti-Rasāyana*, *bhakti* is integrated into the Advaita framework as a *rasa*: a saving aesthesis that liquefies (*drāvayati*) the *citta* into the form of *Bhagavat*. The affective register is preserved without being collapsed into mere preliminary purification. Madhusūdana treats Madhva not as a 'crude dualist' but as a serious dialectical opponent whose arguments demand technical reply.

engaged_works.title: Advaita-Siddhi
engaged_works.ascription_notes: Securely authored; the direct refutation of Vyāsatīrtha's Nyāyāmṛta.
engaged_works.summary: The principal Advaita reply to Vyāsatīrtha's *Nyāyāmṛta* and one of the most technically demanding works in the Sanskrit polemical tradition. Structurally the *Advaita-Siddhi* tracks the *Nyāyāmṛta*'s order, constructing a counter-reply for each anti-Advaita argument: against *anirvacanīya-mithyātva*, against *bhāva-rūpā-avidyā* with Brahman as locus, against the *dṛśyatva-* and *jaḍatva-anumānas*, against the Advaitic analyses of *bheda* and *adhyāsa*. The entire prior Advaita arsenal — Maṇḍana, Sureśvara, Sarvajñātman, Prakāśātman, Vimuktātman, Ānandabodha, Citsukha — is drawn on and refined. The opening *paricheda* offers four successive definitions of *mithyātva* — *sva-āśrayatva-niṣṭha-atyantābhāva-pratiyogitva*; *sva-āśraya-niṣṭhātyanta-abhāva-pratiyogitva* in a second formulation; *sad-vilakṣaṇatva*; and the cancellability-by-*brahma-jñāna* characterisation — each tailored to a distinct Vyāsatīrtha objection. The cumulative point is that *mithyātva* survives multiple non-equivalent characterisations and that none collapses *mithyā* into *asat*. *Bhāva-rūpā-avidyā* with Brahman as locus and object is defended; the *anumānas* for *mithyātva* are rebuilt against Vyāsatīrtha's Nyāya-grade refutations. The exchange across Madhusūdana, Vyāsatīrtha, and Brahmānanda Sarasvatī (*Laghu-Candrikā*) is one of the high-water marks of Indian philosophical debate; both sides are preserved and read together to this day.

engaged_works.title: Siddhanta-Bindu
engaged_works.ascription_notes: Securely authored; gloss on Śaṅkara's Daśa-Ślokī.
engaged_works.summary: Compact independent gloss — *bindu*, 'drop' — on Śaṅkara's *Daśa-Ślokī* (the ten verses summarising Advaita), expanded into a self-contained Advaita primer. Madhusūdana works through the standard Vivaraṇa architecture: Brahman as locus of avidyā, the *avaccheda-* and *pratibimba-vāda* alternatives for the *jīva* (with characteristic non-partisan exposition), *anirvacanīya-bhāva-rūpā-avidyā*, the role of the Mahāvākya in producing *akhaṇḍākāra-vṛtti*, and *jīvanmukti*. The work is one of the standard pedagogical condensations through which advanced students enter the Vivaraṇa technical idiom. Several sub-commentaries — Brahmānanda's *Nyāya-Ratnāvalī*, Pūrṇānanda's *Siddhānta-Bindu-Tippaṇī* — attest its central place in the post-Madhusūdana curriculum.

engaged_works.title: Bhakti-Rasayana
engaged_works.ascription_notes: Securely authored; the Advaita defense of bhakti as a saving aesthesis.
engaged_works.summary: Independent treatise in three chapters — *Bhakti-Rasāyana*, 'the elixir of *bhakti*' — that integrates *bhakti* into the Advaita framework via the *rasa*-theoretic vocabulary of Sanskrit poetics. *Bhakti* is defined as a *rasa*: specifically the *bhagavad-rasa*, a *sthāyibhāva* of love-for-Bhagavat developed through *vibhāva*, *anubhāva*, and *vyabhicāri-bhāva*, whose effect is to liquefy (*drāvayati*) the *citta* into the form (*ākāra*) of Bhagavat. The argument is that *bhakti* is not opposed to Advaita but is its proper aesthesis. *Jñāna* yields *mokṣa*; *bhakti* is itself a *paramapuruṣārtha* of a distinctive kind. The work draws extensively on the *Bhāgavata-Purāṇa* and is in critical conversation with the Gauḍīya analysis of *bhakti-rasa* in Rūpa Gosvāmī's *Bhakti-Rasāmṛta-Sindhu*. Madhusūdana respects the affective phenomenology while subordinating it metaphysically to *brahmātmaikya*. The *Bhakti-Rasāyana* is a key source for an Advaita treatment of devotion that does not collapse it into mere preliminary purification.

engaged_works.title: Gudhartha-Dipika on the Gita
engaged_works.summary: Sustained Advaita Gītā-commentary — 'illumination of the hidden meaning' — that reads the *Bhagavad-Gītā* through the Vivaraṇa lens while preserving the *bhakti*-register defended in the *Bhakti-Rasāyana*. The commentary maps the eighteen chapters onto a three-*kāṇḍa* structure (six chapters of *karma*, six of *bhakti*, six of *jñāna*); treats Kṛṣṇa as *Saguṇa-Brahman* whose recognition as *Nirguṇa-Brahman* is the soteriological climax; and gives technically careful Advaita readings of the controversial verses. BG 18.66's *sarva-dharmān parityajya* is read as *sannyāsa* enabling *jñāna-niṣṭhā*; BG 9 and 12 on *bhakti* are read with full affective seriousness, not reduced to *upāsanā*. The *Gūḍhārtha-Dīpikā* is one of the four classical Advaita Gītā-commentaries, alongside Śaṅkara, Ānandagiri, and Nīlakaṇṭha. It is the standard reference for the post-fifteenth-century Advaita-with-*bhakti* synthesis.

key_passages.locus_long: Siddhānta-Bindu, Upodghāta, on the mahāvākyas as the pramāṇa of ātmatattva
key_passages.sanskrit_iast:
tasya cātmatattvasya "tattvam asi" "ahaṃ brahmāsmi"
ityādivedāntamahāvākyam eva pramāpakam /
vākyaṃ ca padārthajñānadvāreṇaiva jñāpakam iti
tattvampadārthayoḥ prakṛtavākyānukūlayor anyato 'siddhatvāt
tāv api śāstreṇaiva pramātavyau yūpāhavanīyapadārthavat //

pada_analysis.gloss: of the reality of the self
the great Vedāntic sentence beginning thus
only by way of knowledge of the word-meanings
of the meanings of the word 'tat' and the word 'tvam'
suited to the sentence at hand

karaka_structure.note: genitive dependent on ātmatattvasya
genitive governed by the predicate pramāpakam
subject of the implied copula in 'pramāpakam'
instrumental specifying the means by which a sentence reveals
subject of the implied copula in 'jñāpakam'
genitive pair whose referents are to be known
further qualifies the two padārthas
reason: because they are not established from another source
instrumental of the means by which the two padārthas are known

verb_modality.note: finite verb inside the quoted mahāvākya
finite verb inside the quoted mahāvākya

key_passages.english_close: And of that reality of the self, the great Vedāntic sentences 'That thou art' and 'I am Brahman' alone are the means of valid cognition. And a sentence is revelatory only by way of knowledge of the word-meanings; therefore, since the meanings of the words 'tat' and 'tvam', suited to the sentence in question, are not established from elsewhere, those two too must be known from scripture alone, like the meanings of 'yūpa' and 'āhavanīya'.
key_passages.english_paraphrase: The Upaniṣadic identity-sentences are the decisive pramāṇa here, and even the meanings of their key terms have to be learned from śruti rather than from some independent source.

preserved_terms.first_use_gloss: the 'great sentence' of the Upaniṣads that discloses identity
the meaning borne by a word

key_passages.why_this_passage: This is not a detached anthology line but the Upodghāta's programmatic statement of how the whole text will proceed: the mahāvākya is the operative pramāṇa, and its key terms must be interpreted within śruti. That makes it a fair and central witness to Madhusūdana's epistemology of liberation.
key_passages.source_edition: Divānji (ed.), Siddhāntabindu, Baroda: Oriental Institute, 1933; transcribed from the local GRETIL file madhusudana_siddhanta_bindu_gretil.txt.

key_passages.locus_long: Siddhānta-Bindu, Prathamo Vibhāgaḥ, prose under verse 1, defining anirvācyam ajñānam
key_passages.sanskrit_iast:
tasmāt pariśeṣād bhrāntir iyam iti sthite
tatkāraṇam api yogyaṃ kiñcit kalpanīyam /
kalpyamānaṃ ca tadātmany adhyastatayaiva
dharmigrāhakamānena sidhyatīti na jānāmīti
sākṣipratītisiddham anirvācyam ajñānam eva tat /
na cedam abhāvarūpaṃ
jñānasya nityatvena tadabhāvānupapatter uktatvāt //

pada_analysis.morphology: nom sg neut pres pass part

pada_analysis.gloss: by the cognition that grasps the substrate
of the nature of an absence
because the non-existence of that is untenable

karaka_structure.note: locative absolute: 'when this has been settled'
subject within the locative-absolute frame
subject of the gerundive construction 'kalpanīyam'
the 'something' to be posited as cause
locus in which the posited entity is established as superimposed
instrumental of manner: precisely in the mode of superimposition
the cognition by which that superimposed entity is established
genitive governing nityatvena
reason why ajñāna cannot be mere absence
stated reason: the absence of eternal knowledge is untenable

verb_modality.note: first-person cognition quoted in na jānāmīti

key_passages.english_close: Therefore, when it has been established by elimination that this is an error, some suitable cause for it must also be posited. And what is posited is established, precisely as superimposed on that self, by the cognition that grasps the substrate, 'I do not know'; that indeed is indefinable ignorance, established by witness-cognition. And this is not of the nature of an absence, because, knowledge being eternal, the absence of that has already been shown to be untenable.
key_passages.english_paraphrase: Madhusūdana argues that error requires a positive but indefinable ignorance disclosed in the witness-backed awareness 'I do not know'; it cannot be reduced to sheer non-existence.

preserved_terms.first_use_gloss: ignorance, here the explanatory basis of superimposition
indefinable as either simply real or simply unreal
the witnessing consciousness that underlies experience

key_passages.why_this_passage: This compact prose definition comes in the middle of Madhusūdana's actual argument against rival accounts of error and ignorance, not as a later summary slogan. It fairly represents his mature Advaita by making visible the positive, anirvācya status of ajñāna that underwrites superimposition without collapsing mithyā into sheer non-being.
key_passages.source_edition: Divānji (ed.), Siddhāntabindu, Baroda: Oriental Institute, 1933; transcribed from the local GRETIL file madhusudana_siddhanta_bindu_gretil.txt.

key_passages.sanskrit_iast:
nava-rasa-militaṃ vā kevalaṃ vā pumarthaṃ
paramam iha mukunde bhakti-yogaṃ vadanti /
nirupama-sukha-saṃvid-rūpam aspṛṣṭa-duḥkhaṃ
tam aham akhila-tuṣṭyai śāstra-dṛṣṭyā vyanajmi //

pada_analysis.gloss: having the form of incomparable bliss-consciousness

karaka_structure.note: direct object of vadanti, further qualified as the supreme human end
locative specifying the devotional object
speaker of vyanajmi
object of vyanajmi, referring to bhakti-yoga
for whose satisfaction the manifestation is made
instrumental of means or standpoint

key_passages.english_close: They call bhakti-yoga in Mukunda here the supreme human end, either mixed with the nine rasas or by itself, having the form of incomparable bliss-consciousness and untouched by sorrow. That I manifest, by the vision of scripture, for the satisfaction of all.
key_passages.english_paraphrase: The work opens by declaring devotion to Mukunda to be a highest end in its own right, and by promising to establish that claim scripturally.

preserved_terms.first_use_gloss: aesthetic savor, here folded into devotional experience
Kṛṣṇa/Viṣṇu as the giver of liberation

key_passages.why_this_passage: This is the programmatic opening verse of the Bhakti-Rasāyana, so it states the work's thesis rather than a detachable flourish. It is therefore the fairest possible starting point for Madhusūdana's claim that bhakti is a supreme end and not merely a preliminary discipline.
key_passages.source_edition: Janardan Shastri Pandeya (ed.), Śrī-Bhagavad-Bhakti-Rasāyaṇam, Vidyabhavan Prācya-Vidyā-Grantha-Mālā 91, Varanasi: Chowkhamba Vidyabhavan, 3rd ed. 1998; transcribed from the local archive PDF text layer.

key_passages.locus_long: Bhakti-Rasāyana, Prathama Ullāsa, commentary on kārikā 1, distinguishing bhakti from brahma-vidyā
key_passages.sanskrit_iast:
dravī-bhāva-pūrvikā hi manaso bhagavad-ākāratā
savikalpaka-vṛtti-rūpā bhaktiḥ /
dravī-bhāvānupetādvitīyātma-mātra-gocarā
nirvikalpaka-mano-vṛttir brahma-vidyā /
bhagavad-guṇa-garimā-granthana-rūpa-grantha-śravaṇaṃ
bhakti-sādhanam /
tat tvam asyādi vedānta-mahā-vākyaṃ
brahma-vidyā-sādhanam //

pada_analysis.gloss: the state of taking the form of Bhagavat
having the form of a determinate mental modification
having as object only the non-dual self, unaccompanied by melting emotion
hearing a text whose form is the setting forth of the majesty of Bhagavat's qualities

samasa_vigrahas.resolution: dravībhāvena anupetā, advitīya ātmā eva mātraṃ gocaro yasyāḥ sā
samasa_vigrahas.note: stacked compound combining privative qualification with object-specification

samasa_vigrahas.resolution: bhagavato guṇānāṃ garimṇo granthanaṃ rūpaṃ yasya granthasya tat śravaṇam

karaka_structure.note: genitive dependent on bhagavad-ākāratā
subject of the implied copula in the first nominal sentence
subject of the implied copula in the second nominal sentence
subject of the implied copula in 'bhakti-sādhanam'
subject of the implied copula in 'brahma-vidyā-sādhanam'

key_passages.english_close: Bhakti is the mind's taking on the form of Bhagavat, preceded by melting emotion, and having the form of a determinate mental modification. Brahma-vidyā is an indeterminate mental modification, unaccompanied by melting emotion, whose object is only the non-dual self. Hearing a text whose form is the setting forth of the majesty of Bhagavat's qualities is the means of bhakti. The great Vedāntic sentence beginning with 'tat tvam asi' is the means of brahma-vidyā.
key_passages.english_paraphrase: Madhusūdana sharply distinguishes devotional cognition from liberating Brahman-knowledge: they differ in affective tone, intentional object, and textual trigger, even though both matter within his Advaita framework.

preserved_terms.first_use_gloss: the melting or liquefaction of the inner instrument in devotion
a determinate cognition with articulated content
the liberating knowledge whose object is Brahman alone

key_passages.why_this_passage: This prose is one of the clearest places where Madhusūdana explicitly differentiates bhakti from brahma-vidyā in terms of phenomenology, object, and scriptural means. It is therefore representative rather than selective: the passage shows how his Advaita integration of devotion works structurally, not just rhetorically.
key_passages.source_edition: Janardan Shastri Pandeya (ed.), Śrī-Bhagavad-Bhakti-Rasāyaṇam, Vidyabhavan Prācya-Vidyā-Grantha-Mālā 91, Varanasi: Chowkhamba Vidyabhavan, 3rd ed. 1998; transcribed from the local archive PDF text layer.
